cases.name: Robotic direct labeling of hot steel slab
cases.process: After continuous casting the slab surface is about 800℃, covered in scale. A robotic labeler applies the label automatically to the slab end face at the roller table; it travels through the cooling bed, transport and stacking up to furnace entry.
cases.challenge: 800℃ heat + continuously flaking scale + rapid cooling. Ordinary labels instantly carbonize, curl or peel off with the scale; spray marking drops off with the scale; manual tagging needs personnel near the heat.
cases.solution: HP-900 + robotic labeler + on-site thermal transfer print. HP-900 applies at 450–800℃ (max heat resistance 800℃); the heat-activated adhesive bonds on hot contact and the barcode stays scannable. For direct application up to 900℃, use HP-900.

cases.name: Robotic direct labeling of hot steel coil
cases.process: After hot rolling the coil surface is about 550℃; a robot applies the label to the coil eye end face or outer wrap. It follows cooling, banding and warehousing; downstream may include pickling/galvanizing pretreatment.
cases.challenge: 550℃ heat + curved coil surface. The label must conform to the curve without lifting and stay readable through downstream pretreatment.
cases.solution: HP-600 — applies at 250–600℃ (max heat resistance 600℃), stable ink without smudging, scannable while hot; for higher surfaces step up to HP-900 (up to 900℃).

cases.name: Robotic direct labeling of hot steel billet
cases.process: After the caster cut the billet end face is about 750℃. A robot labels it downstream of the cutting machine; the billet cools on the bed, is stacked, and is scanned before furnace entry to confirm identity and feed back to MES.
cases.challenge: The billet was previously identified by a stamped number whose readability is unreliable, causing misidentification; the rough 750℃ scale surface won't hold ordinary labels.
cases.solution: HP-900 — applies at 450–800℃ (max heat resistance 800℃), thermal-shock and scale-flake resistant, roll-fed for automatic labelers; for direct application up to 900℃, use HP-900.

cases.name: Wire pickling + annealing full traceability
cases.process: Wire/bar coils are first pickled (sulfuric/hydrochloric acid) to remove scale, then annealed in a furnace at about 800℃ × 10 hours. The label is applied at room temperature and follows the coil throughout.
cases.challenge: First strong-acid immersion, then prolonged heat — chemical attack and sustained high temperature stack up. Ordinary label stock/ink dissolves or carbonizes and traceability breaks.
cases.solution: HP-M83 — an ultra-high-temp label/tag resisting acid immersion + 800℃ long anneal; barcode still machine-readable after the furnace, giving one continuous chain from pretreatment to heat treatment.

cases.name: Forged part heat-treatment traceability
cases.process: The forging is hot-forged at 800℃, then annealed at 1100℃ × 18 hours. The label must survive extreme heat plus extreme duration and stay readable.
cases.challenge: 1100℃ is extreme, compounded by 18 hours of exposure that even ordinary heat-resistant labels struggle with; a lost mark mixes up forged batches.
cases.solution: HP-L90 — built for extreme forging heat treatment, resisting 1100℃-class prolonged exposure and keeping the barcode readable after annealing.

cases.name: Robotic direct labeling of hot aluminum sow
cases.process: After casting, the aluminum sow surface is about 550℃; a robot applies the label at the roller table, following the sow through cooling, stacking and warehousing.
cases.challenge: 550℃ aluminum surface — fast heat conduction and easy oxidation; the label must bond instantly and resist rapid cooling.
cases.solution: HP-600 — 550℃ direct application, compatible with robotic labeling and on-site thermal transfer print, scannable while hot.

cases.name: Aluminum billet homogenizing management
cases.process: The aluminum billet is at room temperature ~400℃ when labeled, then enters a homogenizing anneal furnace at about 550℃ for several hours, before cutting and extrusion.
cases.challenge: A wide application-temperature span (r.t to 400℃) and it must survive the 550℃ anneal; a lost mark confuses alloy grade / heat batch.
cases.solution: HP-350N / 380N+ / 360 — covering different application temperatures and anneal needs; select per actual conditions to keep traceability through homogenizing.

cases.name: Aluminum heat-treatment jig management
cases.process: Jigs used for solution/aging heat treatment of aluminum parts undergo about 500℃ × several hours × many cycles. The label/tag is fixed to the jig for the long term.
cases.challenge: Jigs re-enter the furnace repeatedly and must stay readable through dozens to hundreds of thermal cycles; single-use labels can't cope.
cases.solution: HP-L90 — an extreme heat-treatment tag for tooling and jig asset management, staying readable through repeated thermal cycles and cycle counting.

cases.name: High-temp ceramic kiln jig management
cases.process: Kiln jigs of alumina, glass or carbon undergo firing cycles of 1000℃ × over 10 hours. The tag is mechanically fixed to the jig and reused.
cases.challenge: 1000℃+ is extreme and exposure exceeds 10 hours; ordinary labels fully ash out, making a readable mark very hard to keep.
cases.solution: HP-CBR CX2 / HB — extreme-high-temp mechanically fixed tags for 1000℃+ prolonged kiln firing.

cases.name: Laboratory crucible heat-treatment mgmt
cases.process: Laboratory/production crucibles undergo 1000℃ × several-hour thermal cycles and need reliable identity marking to track batch and use.
cases.challenge: Crucibles are small, cycled frequently and extremely hot; the mark must withstand 1000℃ and be repeatedly scannable.
cases.solution: HP-CBR CX2 / HB — mechanically fixed tags resisting repeated 1000℃ heat; for direct marking on a crucible body within the direct-application range, use HP-900 (450–900℃ direct application).

products.name: HP-900
products.overview: Heat-resistant labels that can be applied to hot objects such as steel, aluminum or copper immediately after the casting process. Thermal transfer printers allow variable information and barcodes to be easily printed.
installation: Slab, Bloom, Bar, Billet, Plate, Hot Coil
Sow, Ingot, Billet, Slab or Plate
spec: Category: labels for direct hot application

products.name: HP-600
products.overview: A high-temp direct label applied at 250–600℃ (max heat resistance 600℃), designed for objects such as hot coils and aluminum sows; heat-activated adhesive, stable ink at heat, conforms to curves without lifting.
benefits: Print-and-apply while hot; early batch identity
products.application: Robotic labeling at rolling/casting exit → coil eye or sow surface → cooling/banding/stacking → scan-in feedback.

products.name: HP-M83
products.overview: Built for the wire pretreatment chain of 'pickle first, then long anneal', resisting both acid attack and 800℃ × 10h sustained heat; one continuous batch trace from pretreatment to heat treatment.
benefits: Keeps the mark through the most batch-losing step
Scan at furnace exit, data feedback
products.application: Room-temp labeling of coil → pickling bath (sulfuric/hydrochloric) → anneal furnace 800℃×10h → scan at exit → finishing & warehousing.

products.name: HP-350N / 380N+ / 360
products.overview: For aluminum billet homogenizing — applied at r.t to 400℃, then through a ~550℃ anneal for several hours. Three grades cover different application-temperature bands and anneal intensities.
products.application: Billet labeling (r.t~400℃) → 550℃ homogenizing furnace → scan at exit → traceability before cutting/extrusion.

products.name: HP-CBR CX2
products.category: Labels for Heat Treatment / Process Management
products.overview: Track products through heat processes using scannable barcodes. Industries include ceramics, porcelain, concrete, electronics, and a variety of metals.
features: After the baking process, the label becomes integrated with the substrate surface
Good durability against scratching, pickling, impact, and multiple heat cycles
spec: Application temperature: room temperature and above
Category: labels for heat treatment / process management
Industries: ceramics, porcelain, concrete, electronics, metals

products.name: Green-body fired-in mark [model TBC]
products.overview: Applied at the green-body stage (water-laden, soft, deformable); it accompanies the body through drying → bisque/binder burn-out → firing at ~1250–1300℃. Inorganic matter fuses into the glaze, permanently firing the barcode into the ceramic — one-time, indelible, no re-labeling.
features: Applies to wet, porous soft surface
Inorganic matter fuses into glaze, permanent
benefits: Single identity from green body to finished ware, no re-labeling
spec: Bond: inorganic matter fused into glaze
products.application:
Green-body labeling → drying → bisque/binder burn-out → firing 1250–1300℃ → barcode permanently fired into glaze → scan trace.
★ Four challenge gates: ① wet-surface bonding ② synchronized drying shrinkage ③ inorganic matter stays put through burn-out ④ fuses into glaze without distortion.

products.name: HP-L90
products.category: Tags for Heat Treatment / Process Management
products.overview: Heat-resistant tags can be attached to a variety of metal products including hot steel, immediately after casting. Tags can also be designed to survive reheat cycles, pickling baths, acid baths, or galvanizing, thus eliminating the need to re-identify products after these processes.
features: Print and cut in a thermal transfer printer
installation: Jig management for heat treatment process
details: HEATPROOF tags can be attached directly onto hot steel and aluminum immediately after casting, to track products through reheat processes such as annealing or homogenizing.
YStech works with customers to implement identification solutions that meet each company's individual system requirements.
spec: Durability: survives reheat cycles, pickling / acid baths, galvanizing
